In a conservative English rendering (the present author's): *I saw at the same time developing in me, under the influence of habit, a faculty to which I owe the greater part of the observations set down below: that of often having awareness, while asleep, of my true situation, and of keeping then, within the dream, the feeling of my waking preoccupations, and thus retaining enough mastery over my ideas to direct, as needed, their course in whatever direction it suited me to give them.* The terms here are the empirical tradition's central operating concepts: *conscience en dormant* (awareness in sleep, an early phrasing of what van Eeden would call lucidity), the persistence into the dream of waking memory and concern, and what Saint-Denys calls *empire sur mes idées* — a measured kind of authority over the dream's course. He is explicit that this is not a metaphysical claim. He treats the faculty as one that arose with practice and that could in principle be cultivated by others.
He is also honest about the cost. The early phase of the work brought on persistent headaches that forced him to suspend the experiments for a time:

Des maux de tête m'assaillirent, et je crus devoir interrompre mes élucubrations nocturnes ; mais un repos d'esprit relatif m'ayant rendu la santé sans altérer cette faculté définitivement acquired de m'observer parfois en rêvant... (Saint-Denys 1867, p. 7)

(English rendering: *Headaches assailed me, and I judged it necessary to interrupt my nocturnal lucubrations; but a relative rest of mind, having restored my health without altering the now definitively acquired faculty of observing myself within the dream...*) The faculty, once consolidated, persisted for the next twenty years without further trouble, and the book is essentially the record of what he learned in that period: how the dream draws its raw material from waking impressions; how attention can be redirected within a dream; how recurring nightmares can be unlearned; how the apparent hard edge between waking thought and the dream's *marche spontanée des idées* (spontaneous procession of ideas) is in fact porous in both directions.
A useful representative passage on the rhythm of the practice as he describes it elsewhere in the book:

[...] la conscience de ma situation me revient en rêve à peu près une nuit sur deux. Si je m'y attache, pour analyser ou diriger les illusions du songe, je puis la retenir assez longtemps. (Saint-Denys 1867, p. ___ [marker missing in the OCR extract])

(English rendering: *The awareness of my situation returns to me in dream about one night in two. If I attach myself to it, in order to analyze or direct the illusions of the dream, I can hold it for a fair while.*) The frequency is unusual; the patience is the kind that produces the data, and the willingness to attach the attention is the part that the Tibetan tradition (Unit 2) and the LaBerge laboratory tradition (Unit 5) would each independently come to recognize as the practical pivot.
The shape of his project

Saint-Denys is at pains to refuse a metaphysical reading of his own work. The book's full title — *Les Rêves et les moyens de les diriger: observations pratiques* (*Dreams and the Means of Directing Them: Practical Observations*) — announces both its empirical reserve and its practical aim. Where his contemporary Alfred Maury (1817–1892), who had published *Le Sommeil et les Rêves* in 1861, treated dreams as essentially pathological phenomena (a symptom-language of the body and brain), Saint-Denys treats them as observable phenomena of a healthy attention turned inward in a different mode. The Carroy review (which the next section takes up) marks this distinction sharply: Maury and Saint-Denys are the founders of two contrasting nineteenth-century sciences of the dream, and the contrast — the medical-pathological versus the practical-introspective — is the contrast that the rest of this unit's history sits within.

The book had a curious afterlife. It was rare enough by the early twentieth century that, as the title page of the 1867 edition records, "ouvrage devenu assez rare pour que ni Freud ni Havelock Ellis n'aient réussi à en prendre connaissance" — "a work become rare enough that neither Freud nor Havelock Ellis succeeded in getting acquainted with it" (printed material on the source file's title page,, lines 110–125). André Breton, in *Les Vases communicants*, would later note Saint-Denys as "le premier homme à penser qu'il n'était pas impossible... de vaincre à son profit les résistances" within the dream — the first to think that one might, within the dream, work with what otherwise simply happens to one. The Surrealists rediscovered him in the 1920s, the Society for Psychical Research circles via van Eeden, and, much later, the contemporary lucid-dreaming movement; but for most of his own century after publication he was a marginal figure. The Carroy reframing is the historiographic correction that returns him to the center of the story.
II. The Carroy reframing: there was a science before Freud

Jacqueline Carroy's *Nuits savantes: Une histoire des rêves (1800–1945)* (Paris: Éditions de l'EHESS, 2012) is the book that, in the contemporary French historiography of dream studies, has restored to the nineteenth century the empirical phase that Freud's hegemony had obscured. The volume is reviewed in the source bundle in a short notice held. The review sets out Carroy's argument with care.

The review opens by noting that, although the use of *clefs des songes* (dream-keys, or dream-interpretation manuals) goes back to antiquity, "c'est au XIXe siècle que des savants commencent à se pencher sur les rêves en tant qu'objets scientifiques à part entière" — "it is in the nineteenth century that scholars begin to lean over dreams as scientific objects in their own right" (Carroy review,). This empirical phase, the review continues, was conducted largely by men who *se mettent en scène, s'exposent, se révèlent* — who put themselves on stage in their own dream-records, both protagonists and spectator-observers of their own nights. The journals were not private; they were "diffusés et publiés en tant que véritables documents scientifiques" (circulated and published as genuine scientific documents). This is the empirical-introspective tradition before Freud.
The review's account of Saint-Denys is worth quoting: where Maury treats dreams as pathological, Saint-Denys

Chez ce sinologue de renom, qui occupe à partir de 1874 la chaire de tartaro-mandarin au Collège de France, le réalisme des récits nocturnes fait place à une transposition fortement esthétisée. Hervey apparaît ici comme un traducteur infidèle reconstruisant ses matériaux oniriques et prônant par le fait même un nouvel art de rêver — art qui, de par son rejet des conceptions strictement organicistes, séduira notamment l'onirologue Sigmund Freud. (Carroy review, lines 48–53)

(English rendering: *In this renowned sinologist, who from 1874 held the chair of Tartar-Manchurian at the Collège de France, the realism of nocturnal accounts gives way to a strongly aestheticized transposition. Hervey appears here as an unfaithful translator reconstructing his dream materials and, by that fact, advocating a new art of dreaming — an art which, by its rejection of strictly organicist conceptions, will seduce the dream-theorist Sigmund Freud.*) The review's claim is twofold: first, that Saint-Denys's empirical work was already proposing a "new art of dreaming" — a *praxis*, not just an observational record; and second, that Freud was a more attentive reader of Saint-Denys than the standard historiography has acknowledged. The point is sharpened later: Carroy holds, the reviewer reports, that "la formation française en psychopathologie [de Freud] a fait l'objet de nombreuses études, mais [l]es lectures françaises ou francophones sur les rêves ont été peu étudiées" (Freud's French training in psychopathology has been much studied, but his French-language reading on dreams has been little studied), and that "c'est en immergeant résolument [la *Traumdeutung*] dans la science des rêves de son temps que l'on peut mieux saisir au bout du compte son originalité" — it is by resolutely immersing the *Interpretation of Dreams* in the dream-science of its time that one can finally better grasp its originality.

What follows from this reframing, for the purposes of the present unit, is a corrective: the standard story in which "the West rediscovered what Asia knew" by way of LaBerge in the 1980s, and in which Freud was the inaugurator of modern dream science, both flatten the actual historical record. Saint-Denys, Maury, Tarde, and the broader mid-nineteenth-century European tradition were doing serious empirical dream science a generation before Freud, and the lucid-dream practitioners who came after — van Eeden, Arnold-Forster, and (much later) the Tholey/Hearne/LaBerge tradition — were extending a project Freud had not started but had absorbed.
III. Frederik van Eeden (1913): the term "lucid dream" enters English
The 1913 paper to the SPR

Frederik van Eeden (1860–1932) was a Dutch psychiatrist, novelist, and reformer who, in 1913, presented a paper to the Society for Psychical Research titled simply "A Study of Dreams." The paper is held in the source bundle and is short enough to read in a sitting. It is the document in which the term *lucid dream* (with its present meaning) enters the English-language scientific vocabulary.

Van Eeden's project is empirical in the same Saint-Denys mode: he reports that since 1896 he had kept a journal of his own dreams; that since 1898 he had kept a separate record of one particular kind of dream, of which by 1913 he had collected 352 instances; and that the body of work he had to offer was "only a preliminary sketch, a short announcement of a greater work" he hoped to complete in later years (van Eeden 1913, opening). What he then offers is a typology — a classification of dreams into nine kinds — and the bulk of the paper is given over to careful description of each.
The seventh kind is the kind he had been collecting for fifteen years:

The seventh type of dreams, which I call lucid dreams, seems to me the most interesting and worthy of the most careful observation and study. Of this type I experienced and wrote down 352 cases in the period between January 20, 1898, and December 26, 1912. In these lucid dreams the reintegration of the psychic functions is so complete that the sleeper remembers day-life and his own condition, reaches a state of perfect awareness, and is able to direct his attention, and to attempt different acts of free volition. Yet the sleep, as I am able confidently to state, is undisturbed, deep and refreshing. (van Eeden 1913)

That is the canonical definition. Two features of it are worth marking. First, van Eeden's emphasis on *reintegration*: the lucid dream is not a state of partial sleep, in his account, but a state in which the psychic functions, dissociated by ordinary sleep, recombine in a different mode. He resists Havelock Ellis's reading (that the lucid state is a sort of hypnagogic trance rather than a "real" dream) by pointing out that what he is reporting was observed during deep healthy sleep, with full recollection of day-life, and that whatever it is called, this state is what he had been studying. Second, his explicit naming of the empirical concept: the term is offered as a stipulation for a phenomenon the reader can verify against the data. Van Eeden makes no metaphysical claim about a "dream-body" except in the experiential sense — the sense that, within the lucid dream, "the sensation of having a body — having eyes, hands, a mouth that speaks, and so on — is perfectly distinct; yet I know at the same time that the physical body is sleeping and has quite a different position" — an observation that the laboratory eye-signal experiments of the 1970s would, in their own register, eventually confirm.
The first lucid dream van Eeden records
He dates his first glimpse of the state to June 1897, in a passage worth quoting because it exemplifies the empirical reserve of the tradition:

I obtained my first glimpse of this lucidity during sleep in June, 1897, in the following way. I dreamt that I was floating through a landscape with bare trees, knowing that it was April, and I remarked that the perspective of the branches and twigs changed quite naturally. Then I made the reflection, during sleep, that my fancy would never be able to invent or to make an image as intricate as the perspective movement of little twigs seen in floating by. (van Eeden 1913)

The recognition is the practice. He notices, within the dream, the texture of the perceptual data, and the noticing of it carries the inference that this is something his ordinary fancy could not have constructed — and from that inference, awareness of the state. He compares his own approach with a similar observation made independently by Ernst Mach, with the difference that "Mach came to the conclusion that he was dreaming, but it was because he saw the movement of the twigs to be defective, while I had wondered at the naturalness which my fancy could never invent."

In the months following, van Eeden made a deliberate effort to repeat the observation. The second instance, dated to January 1898, has him "lying in the garden before the windows of my study, and saw the eyes of my dog through the glass pane. I was lying on my chest and observing the dog very keenly. At the same time, however, I knew with perfect certainty that I was dreaming and lying on my back in my bed." He resolves to wake up slowly and to observe the transition from the dream-body to the waking body, and reports that "the transition — which I have since undergone many times — is most wonderful. It is like the feeling of slipping from one body into another, and there is distinctly a double recollection of the two bodies." It is the same observation Saint-Denys had been making, expressed in a slightly different vocabulary, and arrived at independently.
The typology, briefly

Van Eeden's nine-type classification is detailed and not all of it bears directly on this unit. The types are: (1) initial dreams, the rare type that occurs at the boundary of waking and sleep; (2) pathological dreams, those caused by fever, indigestion, or other bodily disturbance; (3) ordinary dreaming, the absurd-and-confused type that for many people is the only type they know; (4) vivid dreaming, intense and well-remembered; (5) symbolic or "mocking" dreams, which van Eeden assigns to a category he labels demoniacal, by which he means dreams that have the texture of being arranged by an intelligence of low moral order — his polemical category against Freud's reading of dream symbolism; (6) general dream-sensations, dreams without imagery in which the sleeping mind is occupied across the night by a single person or idea; (7) lucid dreams; (8) demon-dreams, in which the figures van Eeden calls demons appear directly; and (9) the "wrong waking up" type, in which one dreams of waking and only slowly realizes one is still asleep — what later writers have sometimes called false awakening.

The typology is useful here as evidence of the empirical mode in which van Eeden is working: the classifications are not theoretical posits but reports of what his journal contained. A reader of the contemporary lucid-dream literature will recognize most of his types under different names. The "wrong waking up" type is the false awakening of the present-day clinical literature; the lucid type is what LaBerge would test in 1980.

What van Eeden hands forward to the rest of this history, then, is twofold: a name for the central state, and a typology of contiguous states that the practitioner can expect to encounter and need to distinguish.
IV. Mary Arnold-Forster (1921): the first instructional approach

Mary Arnold-Forster's *Studies in Dreams* (London: George Allen & Unwin, 1921) is held in the source bundle. Where Saint-Denys produced an introspective record and van Eeden a typology, Arnold-Forster produced something the tradition had not yet had: a practical manual. The book opens with a foreword by Morton Prince (the American psychologist who had earlier written *The Dissociation of a Personality*), in which Prince says of her that "the accurate recording of observations accurately made is the basis of [science] and requires the development and possession of no common talent" — a measured endorsement that tells the reader what Arnold-Forster's project actually is.
Her preface is unguarded about the book's aim:

By rights, perhaps, the task of an unscientific recorder of dreams should be simply to explore — to record his experiences in the dream state; leaving it to science to explain the whys and wherefores of what has been observed. But it is very difficult for one who has dreamed much, and thought much, and read much, about dreaming not to stray a little beyond the proper province of the recorder, and not to attempt some partial explanation of some of the riddles that are met with. (Arnold-Forster 1921, Preface)

What follows is a chapter-by-chapter exposition of methods. The chapter titles alone are diagnostic: Introductory (the state of dream studies); Dream Control; Flying Dreams; Dream Recording; and onward through more specialized topics. The book's central methodological contribution sits in chapter II, "Dream Control," and in chapter IV, "Dream Recording." The ambition of the chapter on dream control is announced plainly:

I believe, in short, that we can at will stop the recurrence of "bad" dreams, or of dreams that we dislike or dread, and that we can, to a considerable extent, alter the very nature of our dreams by using in our sleep the same faculty of rational selection and rejection that we use with regard to our thoughts and to our wandering fancies by day. (Arnold-Forster 1921, Chapter II)

The proposed method is straightforward, and is given in the form of a *formula* — a sentence or two, repeated during the day and on going to bed, that becomes (with practice) the cue by which the dream mind orients itself within a recurring distressing dream. Her own formula she gives in full: *Remember this is a dream. You are to dream no longer.* The mechanism, as she describes it, is simple recurrence: "I tried repeating this formula to myself from time to time, during the day and on going to bed, always in the same words... until, I suppose, the suggestion that I wanted to imprint upon the dream mind became more definite and more powerful than the impression of any dream; so that when a dream of distress begins to trouble me, the oft-repeated formula is automatically suggested" (Arnold-Forster 1921, Chapter II).

The reader of the contemporary literature will recognize, in this 1921 passage, the kernel of what LaBerge would later formalize as MILD (Mnemonic Induction of Lucid Dreams). Arnold-Forster's formula functions as exactly what MILD would later prescribe: a pre-sleep intention, embedded in a memorable phrase, whose repeated rehearsal gradually trains the sleeping mind to recognize the dream state as a dream state. She is not framing her method as an induction technique for lucid dreaming — her stated aim is the elimination of bad dreams. But the mechanism she has discovered is general, and her own dream records show the same. The chapter on "voluntary dreaming" extends the same formula in the positive direction: setting a pre-sleep intention to fly, or to revisit a particular landscape, or to find oneself in a particular dream-room.

A sustained passage from her chapter on dream recording is also worth marking — both for what it shares with the present-day dream-journaling literature and for the unguarded interest in dream cultivation as a practice of attention worth cultivating for its own sake:

Sleep which brings us our dreams fulfils the eternal need within us, the need of romance, the need of adventure; for sleep is the gate which lets us slip through into the enchanted country that lies beyond. We give too little heed to the nightly miracle of our dreams. Long hours are spent by us in dreaming sleep, during which we are absorbed in a life which is for the most part forgotten or ignored by our waking consciousness. (Arnold-Forster 1921, Preface)

The voice here is the voice of a serious self-observer who is not pretending to be a scientist and who is not trying to interpret the dream into anything it is not. The empirical-introspective tradition reaches its practical maturity in this book. Sixty years would pass before LaBerge's laboratory work picked up the same questions on instruments; and when it did, the methods it endorsed were broadly continuous with the ones Arnold-Forster had reported.

A short historical note. Arnold-Forster (1857–1951) was the wife of the British politician Hugh Oakeley Arnold-Forster, and as the foreword suggests, she presented her work in the diffident register that women writing as amateurs in the early twentieth century were expected to adopt. The diffidence has the secondary effect, now, of having concealed how directly her work prefigures both Tholey's reflection technique (1959 onward) and LaBerge's MILD (1980 onward). The Carroy reframing of Saint-Denys applies, by extension, to Arnold-Forster: there was a serious empirical tradition before Freud's Vienna and after his ascent, and the work of recovering it is still in progress.
V. Jung's mature theory of the dream

Jung's relation to the empirical-introspective tradition is more complicated than the relation Saint-Denys, van Eeden, and Arnold-Forster had to one another. Jung is interested in the dream not as an empirical object but as a psychological statement about the dreamer's situation: a function of the unconscious, with specifiable structural relations to the conscious mind. His mature theoretical position on dream is given in the essay "On the Nature of Dreams" (first published 1945 in the *Ciba-Zeitschrift*; revised 1948 for *Über psychische Energetik und das Wesen der Träume*; collected in *The Structure and Dynamics of the Psyche*). The source bundle has the essay. Read alongside the dream-narrative passages of *Memories, Dreams, Reflections* (Jung 1961, ed. Aniela Jaffé; source), the essay gives a clean statement of how Jung's theoretical and practical positions had settled by the last phase of his life.
The dream as compensation

Jung's central thesis in "On the Nature of Dreams" is that the dream is "a fragment of involuntary psychic activity, just conscious enough to be reproducible in the waking state," and that its function in relation to the conscious mind is *compensatory*. He develops the notion against Freud's wish-fulfillment model, but with care to distinguish his concept of compensation from Freud's:

Since the meaning of most dreams is not in accord with the tendencies of the conscious mind but shows peculiar deviations, we must assume that the unconscious, the matrix of dreams, has an independent function. This is what I call the autonomy of the unconscious. The dream not only fails to obey our will but very often stands in flagrant opposition to our conscious intentions. The opposition need not always be so marked; sometimes the dream deviates only a little from the conscious attitude and introduces only slight modifications; occasionally it may even coincide with conscious contents and tendencies. When I attempted to express this behaviour in a formula, the concept of compensation seemed to me the only adequate one, for it alone is capable of summing up all the various ways in which a dream behaves. Compensation must be strictly distinguished from complementation. The concept of a complement is too narrow and too restricting; it does not suffice to explain the function of dreams, because it designates a relationship in which two things supplement one another more or less mechanically. Compensation, on the other hand, as the term implies, means balancing and comparing different data or points of view so as to produce an adjustment or a rectification. (Jung, "On the Nature of Dreams")

The passage is the load-bearing one. Jung's compensation is not a balancing in the mechanical sense (the dream is not the symmetrical opposite of the waking attitude); it is a balancing in an evaluative sense (the dream brings the relevant counterweight to whatever the conscious attitude has pushed too far). Jung lays out three cases: if the conscious attitude is "in large degree one-sided," the dream takes the opposite side; if the conscious attitude is "fairly near the middle," the dream offers slight variations; and if the conscious attitude is "correct," the dream coincides with and emphasizes the tendency, "though without forfeiting its peculiar autonomy." The third case is methodologically important — it is what prevents the compensatory theory from collapsing into the bromide that the dream just says the opposite of what the dreamer thinks.

The dream-interpretation method Jung commends — *taking up the context* — follows from the theory. The dream is not interpreted by reference to a fixed dream-key (as in the older tradition of *clefs des songes*) but by patient inquiry into what each salient feature of the dream meant for the dreamer:

On the basis of these conclusions and for the purpose of ascertaining the meaning of the dream, I have developed a procedure which I call "taking up the context." This consists in making sure that every shade of meaning which each salient feature of the dream has for the dreamer is determined by the associations of the dreamer himself. I therefore proceed in the same way as I would in deciphering a difficult text. (Jung, "On the Nature of Dreams")

The practice is austere. Jung writes that, when a patient tells him a dream and asks his opinion, he has made it a rule "to say first of all to myself: 'I have no idea what this dream means.' After that I can begin to examine the dream." The dream is to be approached without theory and without preconception; the theory comes only in the second pass, after the context has been taken up. The methodological humility is the part of Jung's practice that is most easily overlooked by readers familiar only with the popular caricature of Jungian dream-interpretation as a key-and-lock matching of dream-symbols to archetypes.
"Big" and "little" dreams

The compensatory theory accommodates routine dreams — the "little" dreams Jung says even primitive cultures have distinguished from "big" or "significant" dreams. Big dreams "are often remembered for a lifetime, and not infrequently prove to be the richest jewel in the treasure-house of psychic experience." They tend to occur "during the critical phases of life, in early youth, puberty, at the onset of middle age (thirty-six to forty), and within sight of death." The category is what allows Jung to do the work he does in the alchemical and individuation-oriented later writings: the big dream is a dream in which the contents come not from the personal unconscious only but from a deeper layer Jung calls the collective unconscious, and these contents arrange themselves in long dream-series into what he names *the individuation process*.
The collective layer is what makes the big dream "archetypal" in Jung's sense:

Significant dreams, on the other hand, are often remembered for a lifetime... I have examined many such dreams, and often found in them a peculiarity which distinguishes them from other dreams; they contain symbolical images which we also come across in the mental history of mankind. It is worth noting that the dreamer does not need to have any inkling of the existence of such parallels. This peculiarity is characteristic of dreams of the individuation process, where we find the mythological motifs or mythologems I have designated as archetypes. (Jung, "On the Nature of Dreams")

The methodological move that follows is the controversial one: Jung holds that to interpret a "big" dream, one must have access to the materials of mythology, folklore, and comparative religion, because the dream's symbolic vocabulary is shared with those traditions and not invented privately by the dreamer. This is what gave Jungian dream interpretation its later academic status as a hybrid practice — half clinical, half mythographic.
Two dreams Jung tells about himself

The theoretical positions are more vivid when read alongside the dream-narratives Jung gives of himself in *Memories, Dreams, Reflections*. Two are worth pulling forward: the knight dream (recounted in the chapter on his break with Freud) and the Liverpool dream (in the chapter "Confrontation with the Unconscious").

The first comes after Jung has had a dream in which Freud appears as "a peevish official of the Imperial Austrian monarchy, as a defunct and still walking ghost of a customs inspector" — a figure Jung reads as a compensatory image for his own too-deferential conscious attitude toward Freud at the time. The second part of the same dream is the part with the knight:

Suddenly to see in a modern city, during the noonday rush hour, a crusader coming toward me. What struck me as particularly odd was that none of the many persons walking about seemed to notice him. No one turned his head or gazed after him. It was as though he were completely invisible to everyone but me. I asked myself what this apparition meant, and then it was as if someone answered me but there was no one there to speak: "Yes, this is a regular apparition. The knight always passes by here between twelve and one o'clock, and has been doing so for a very long time [for centuries, I gathered] and everyone knows about it." (*Memories, Dreams, Reflections*, "Sigmund Freud," p. 164)

Jung's reading of the dream, given some pages later, is that the knight belonged to the twelfth century — the period when alchemy was beginning and when the Grail-quest was first being told — and that the dream was therefore announcing his "own world, which had scarcely anything to do with Freud's." The Grail-stories had been important to him since he had read them at fifteen. The dream is treated, in characteristic Jung fashion, as the unconscious's autonomous report on his actual situation: not what he consciously thought (that he was Freud's most promising follower), but what was actually working in him (that his vocation lay elsewhere, in territory Freud's framework could not reach).
The Liverpool dream, much later, is the dream Jung treats as the climactic statement of his individuation process:

I was in Liverpool. With a number of Swiss — say, half a dozen — I walked through the dark streets. I had the feeling that there we were coming from the harbor, and that the real city was actually up above, on the cliffs. We climbed up there.... When we reached the plateau, we found a broad square dimly illuminated by street lights, into which many streets converged. The various quarters of the city were arranged radially around the square. In the center was a round pool, and in the middle of it a small island. While everything round about was obscured by rain, fog, smoke, and dimly lit darkness, the little island blazed with sunlight. On it stood a single tree, a magnolia, in a shower of reddish blossoms. It was as though the tree stood in the sunlight and were at the same time the source of light. (*Memories, Dreams, Reflections*, "Confrontation with the Unconscious," p. 198)

He gives the reading himself: "Liverpool is the 'pool of life.' The 'liver,' according to an old view, is the seat of life — that which 'makes to live.' This dream brought with it a sense of finality. I saw that here the goal had been revealed. One could not go beyond the center. The center is the goal, and everything is directed toward that center. Through this dream I understood that the self is the principle and archetype of orientation and meaning."

The two dreams together exemplify Jung's compensatory theory in operation: the knight dream supplies the figure that Jung's ordinary professional consciousness was not yet entertaining (his vocation lay outside the territory Freud occupied); the Liverpool dream supplies the figure that organizes the work he had been doing for two decades into a single image (the magnolia at the center of the radial city). Neither dream is read as a wish-fulfillment in Freud's sense, and neither is treated as a private code to be unlocked by a fixed dictionary. Both are taken seriously as autonomous productions of an unconscious psyche that, on Jung's account, has its own purposes and its own structural commitments.

The point worth carrying forward to Hillman, in the next section, is that even Jung — for whom the dream is finally the *via regia* to the unconscious in service of the dayworld self's individuation — is taking the dream seriously *on something like its own terms*. Whether the dream's terms are really compatible with the individuation framework is the question Hillman would put.
VI. James Hillman: the archetypal pushback

James Hillman's *The Dream and the Underworld* (New York: Harper & Row, 1979). The extract's opening pages contain the book's central argument with characteristic compression. Hillman writes from inside the Jungian tradition, but the book's purpose is to push back on the use to which that tradition had begun to put the dream by the late 1970s.
The pushback is announced in the choice of title. Hillman writes:

Among the possible titles for this work have been "The Dream Bridge," or "The Dream Between World and Underworld." Freud had already used a similar metaphor; he called the dream a royal road, the via regia to the unconscious. But because this via regia, in most psychotherapy since his time, has become a straight one-way street of all morning traffic, moving out of the unconscious toward the ego's city, I have chosen to face the other way. Hence my title, which is a directional signpost for a different one-way movement, let us say vesperal, into the dark. (Hillman, *The Dream and the Underworld*, "Bridge")

The complaint is that depth psychology, for all its talk of taking the dream seriously, has used the dream as a piece of evidence in service of the ego's own purposes — as a means of gaining "more consciousness about living." Hillman's pushback is that this is the wrong direction of travel:

This little book attempts a different view of the dream from those we are used to. Its thesis does not rely on ideas of repression (Freud) or of compensation (Jung), but imagines dreams in relation with soul and soul with death. I have come to believe that the entire procedure of dream interpretation aiming at more consciousness about living is radically wrong. And I mean "wrong" in all its fullness: harmful, twisted, deceptive, inadequate, mistaken, and exegetically insulting to its material, the dream. When we wrong the dream, we wrong the soul, and if the soul has the intimate connection with death that tradition has always supposed, then mistaken dream interpretation deceives our dying. (Hillman, "Bridge")

The mythological grounding Hillman gives to the pushback is the imagery of the underworld: dreams belong, in the older mythological vocabularies, to the underworld and its gods (Hades, Persephone, the realm of the shades), and an honest dream practice should not pull the dream up into the dayworld's light. The practitioner should go down to meet the dream, rather than calling the dream up to be interpreted on terms the dayworld already accepts:

What is new in what follows is the attempt to re-vision the dream in the light of myth.... It is no secret that dreams belong to the underworld and its Gods, for our title announces this, and the short second chapter shows that a mythic base for dreams is an old story.... We are saying a psychology of dreams that tries to keep a sense of the underworld always present in our work with them. (Hillman, "Bridge")

The full case Hillman makes is longer and richer than the extract here can show, but the kernel is in these passages. For the purposes of the present unit it is enough to record what Hillman is correcting against. The empirical-introspective tradition (Saint-Denys, van Eeden, Arnold-Forster) had treated the dream as a phenomenon to be observed and, where possible, gently directed; the depth-psychological tradition (Jung) had treated the dream as a compensatory function in service of the individuation process. Both traditions, in Hillman's reading, had assumed that the dream was *for* something — for self-knowledge, for therapeutic gain, for the integration of the personality. Hillman's correction is to say that the dream is not for anything in that sense; the dream belongs to its own mythic register and the practitioner's task is to learn to inhabit the register, not to translate its contents into dayworld currency.

The correction is in productive tension, not flat opposition, with both prior traditions. A patient empirical journal of the Saint-Denys kind can still be kept in good faith if the journaler understands that the dreams will not yield up an "interpretation" in the instrumental sense; a Jungian compensatory reading can still be commenced if the analyst understands that the compensation is a conversation with a region of the psyche that has its own integrity and its own resistance to the dayworld's purposes. What Hillman supplies, looking forward to the rest of this history, is an intellectual brake against the bromide that any of this — empirical, depth-psychological, lucid-dream-experimental — is finally for the dayworld self's gain.

The pushback is a useful one to keep in view as the next unit (Modern Scientific Era) takes up LaBerge's laboratory work and the lucid-dream movement that became, in the 1990s, an applied practice. The applied practice is at its best when it remembers that the dream still belongs, on Hillman's reading, to a register that is not the laboratory's.
VII. What this Western pre-history hands forward
The Western rediscovery covered in this unit hands forward four things to the lucid-dream science (Unit 5) and to the contemporary contemplative-scientific dialogue (Unit 6).

First, a vocabulary. *Conscience en dormant* (Saint-Denys) becomes *lucid dream* (van Eeden) becomes the canonical term of the laboratory and contemporary contemplative literatures. The terminology is not neutral — it carries the empirical-introspective tradition's assumption that the relevant variable is awareness, and that awareness is the thing to be cultivated and measured. This is the assumption the Tibetan traditions (Unit 2) share without sharing the framing.

Second, a method. Arnold-Forster's repeated formula, given in 1921 as a technique for the elimination of bad dreams, is the same formula that LaBerge would later codify as MILD. The method works by cultivating, through repetition during the day and at sleep onset, a phrase whose recurrence in the sleeping mind brings the sleeper to recognize the dream as a dream. The mechanism — pre-sleep intention, embedded in memorable language, internalized through patient repetition — is general; the empirical tradition arrived at it independently of the Tibetan tradition that had been using analogous methods for centuries.

Third, an empirical record. Saint-Denys had 352 lucid-dream entries by 1898; van Eeden had 352 by 1912; Arnold-Forster had a journal extending across decades. The records were keptcarefully enough that, by the 1970s, when Keith Hearne and Stephen LaBerge began designing the eye-signal experiments, the prior empirical literature was already substantial. The eye-signal verification did not establish that lucid dreams happened; the empirical-introspective tradition had already established that. The verification established that the phenomenon occurred during physiologically measurable REM sleep — a different and narrower claim.

Fourth, a depth-psychological frame. Jung's compensatory theory and the Hillman correction together set the conceptual frame within which the contemporary practitioner works. The compensatory reading of dream — as the unconscious's autonomous statement on the conscious situation, calling out the dayworld's one-sidedness — is now the default register in which most therapeutic dream work in the West is conducted. The Hillman correction is the in-house critique that prevents the compensatory framework from collapsing into instrumentalism. Both belong to the practitioner's working vocabulary now.

A caution worth marking. The easy formula — "the West rediscovered what Asia knew" — is wrong in two ways. First, the chronology is wrong: the European empirical-introspective tradition was up and running in 1867, three decades before any substantial Tibetan dream-yoga teaching reached the West (the Evans-Wentz publication of 1935 is the first to do so for any sizable readership, and even there the lucid-dream literatures were independent of it). Second, the framing is wrong: the European tradition was working out a phenomenology and a method for cultivating awareness within the dream; the Tibetan tradition was working out a soteriology in which the dream-state was one site within a much larger contemplative discipline whose aim was the recognition of the illusory nature of all experience. The two arcs converge on a method (cultivating awareness in dream) but diverge on the aim (psychological integration vs. recognition of mind's nature). The convergence on method is genuine and worth marking; the divergence on aim is what Unit 6 takes up.

Quotations have been given in French with conservative literal English renderings; the renderings are the present author's. A scholarly edition for publication would substitute Carrington's English translation (where extant) or commission a fresh translation.

The Carroy review** is short and clean. The reviewer's name is not preserved in the extract (only the journal context is recoverable from the title). For citation in the published version this should be filled in.
Van Eeden 1913** is clean; pagination follows the *Proceedings of the Society for Psychical Research*, vol. 26.
Arnold-Forster 1921** is mostly clean; OCR damage at line breaks and around footnotes occasionally corrupts a word, and silent corrections have been made where the meaning was unambiguous.
Jung "On the Nature of Dreams"** is clean enough to quote verbatim; the source text is the Hull translation in *The Structure and Dynamics of the Psyche* (Bollingen series).

Jung *Memories, Dreams, Reflections*** has some OCR damage at chapter heads and around unusual proper names. The dream-narrative passages quoted (the knight dream, the Liverpool dream) survived cleanly. Passages have been silently de-mangled where the meaning was unambiguous.

Hillman *The Dream and the Underworld*** is a short extract (the opening "Bridge" section); the OCR in this extract is partially damaged with frequent spurious line breaks and missing words, but the load-bearing passages quoted above survived legibly.

blocks.text: The main gaps the bundle does not cover, but which a published version of this unit should address: Freud's *Interpretation of Dreams* (1900) is referenced repeatedly above but no source text is in the bundle; the Tholey reflection technique (1959 onward) is mentioned in passing but no Tholey source is in the bundle; and the broader continental phenomenological literature on dream (Bachelard, Binswanger, Boss) is named in the prior archive but is not in the current bundle. Each of these is a natural extension once the corresponding texts are added to the source library.

articles.label: Modern Scientific Era
articles.title: Unit 5 — The Modern Scientific Era
articles.subtitle: The discovery of REM sleep and its dream correlate; the laboratory verification of lucid dreaming through eye-movement signaling; the practical synthesis that put induction techniques in the hands of ordinary readers; and the pharmacological and neuroscientific extensions of the last two decades.
articles.kicker: From Aserinsky and Kleitman's REM paper to the eye-signal verification of lucid dreaming and the contemporary neuroscience of dreams.

blocks.text: What this unit is for

The Asian contemplative dream traditions (Units 1 and 2) and the European philosophical-clinical tradition that came forward in the nineteenth century (Unit 4) developed without empirical access to their central claim. A practitioner could know from inside that he had become aware within a dream and acted with intention there. An outside observer was not in a position to confirm it. There was no trace on any instrument by which the lucid moment could be distinguished from the surrounding sleep. The contemplative literatures and the early Western lucid-dream literatures had to be taken on the testimony of those who had done the work. The science could not yet weigh in.

Two things had to happen before that could change. First, sleep had to be found to have a structure — particular periods, with particular physiological signatures, in which dreaming was concentrated. That happened in 1953, in the work of Eugene Aserinsky and Nathaniel Kleitman at Chicago. Second, a way had to be found for the dreamer, while in the dream, to communicate something to the outside that could not have been faked or post-hoc reconstructed. That happened in the late 1970s, simultaneously and independently in Hull and at Stanford, in the work of Keith Hearne and Stephen LaBerge respectively, when both researchers realized that the eye movements that named REM sleep could be deliberately patterned by a lucid dreamer to send a signal across the sleep-wake threshold. Once that demonstration had been made, a research field became possible, and within a decade the field had produced both a body of laboratory results and a popular practical synthesis — Stephen LaBerge and Howard Rheingold's *Exploring the World of Lucid Dreaming* — that made the subject available to ordinary readers. The pharmacological and neuroscientific extensions of the 2010s have refined the picture further; the central practical and philosophical structure was in place by 1990.

This unit tells that story directly from the surviving primary documents the project bundle holds: the original Aserinsky-Kleitman paper, the LaBerge-Rheingold book, and the 2018 PLOS ONE paper on galantamine. Where the secondary literature appears — Hearne, Tholey, Voss, Dresler, Holzinger — it appears through the source bundle's discussion of those figures. Gaps where the bundle is thin are flagged honestly.
I. The discovery of REM (1953)
A two-page paper

The modern scientific study of dreaming begins with a paper of fewer than two thousand words on two facing pages of *Science*. The paper is titled "Regularly Occurring Periods of Eye Motility, and Concomitant Phenomena, During Sleep," its authors are Eugene Aserinsky, then a graduate student in physiology, and Nathaniel Kleitman, the older man's mentor and head of the sleep laboratory at the University of Chicago, and its date is September 4, 1953 (Aserinsky and Kleitman 1953,). The paper is short because the finding is simple and the case is largely visible in the figure. It describes a phenomenon — bursts of rapid, jerky eye movements during sleep — and reports three things about it: how it was recorded, when in the night it occurred, and what the sleeper was experiencing at the time.

Slow rolling eye movements during sleep had been noted before, the paper concedes at the opening, citing Pietrusky, De Toni, Fuchs and Wu, Andreev, and Burford. "However, this report deals with a different type of eye movement — rapid, jerky, and binocularly symmetrical." The new technique was electrooculography. One pair of leads was placed on the superior and inferior orbital ridges of one eye to detect vertical-plane changes in the corneo-retinal potential; another pair, on the inner and outer canthi of the same eye, to pick up "mainly the horizontal component of eye movement." The recordings were complicated by superfluous potentials from brain waves, lid and jaw muscle activity, and electrode movement. To distinguish the eye signal from this noise the experimenters added a simultaneous monopolar recording from the frontal area, gain-adjusted so that "an equal excursion of both pens signified that the bipolar potential was actually 4 times greater than the monopolar potential." The criterion was further confirmed "by direct observation of several subjects under both weak and gradually intensified illumination," and "motion pictures were taken of 2 subjects without awakening them."

What the recordings showed, in twenty normal adult subjects across several series of experiments, was that this rapid eye motility was not a random or rare event. It occurred in regular periods through the night, separated by long stretches of "ocular quiescence." The mean delay from going to bed to the first appearance of the rapid pattern was three hours fourteen minutes, after which the pattern recurred at intervals of around two hours, with a third or fourth period clustering closer to the end of the night. Each period varied in duration but typically lasted about twenty minutes. The brain wave records during these eye-movement periods were of low amplitude and irregular frequency — a different EEG pattern from the one seen during quiescent sleep — and the bursts of eye motility were accompanied by mild elevation in respiratory rate (16.9 per minute during eye motility versus 13.4 during ocular quiescence, "statistically significant with a probability of less than 0.001"), and by peaks of overt body movement.
The dream correlation

What gives the paper its weight, beyond the phenomenology of the eye movement itself, is the third experiment. To confirm what the authors call "the conjecture that this particular eye activity was associated with dreaming," ten subjects in fourteen experiments were awakened during eye motility and during periods of "at least 30 min to 3 hr of ocular quiescence." The quiescence intervals were chosen on the basis of the EEG to represent "as closely as possible, a depth of sleep comparable to that present during ocular motility" — a control condition. The result is reported in two sentences:

"Of 27 interrogations during ocular motility, 20 revealed detailed dreams usually involving visual imagery; the replies to the remaining 7 queries included complete failure of recall, or else, 'the feeling of having dreamed,' but with inability to recollect any detail of the dream. Of 23 interrogations during ocular inactivity, 19 disclosed complete failure of recall, while the remaining 4 were evenly divided into the other 2 categories." (Aserinsky and Kleitman 1953)

The authors' inference is cautious — they note "the inadequacies of employing a χ² test for the independence of the 2 groups of interrogations" — but the chi-squared test gave a probability "less than 0.01" that the association was due to chance. The paper concludes that "the fact that these eye movements, EEG pattern, and autonomic nervous system activity are significantly related and do not occur randomly suggests that these physiological phenomena, and probably dreaming, are very likely all manifestations of a particular level of cortical activity which is encountered normally during sleep."

That sentence is the founding sentence of the modern science of dreaming. Until it was written, sleep had been treated, as LaBerge and Rheingold put it some forty years later, as "a uniform state of passive withdrawal from the world". After it, sleep was a structured cycle of distinct states with distinct neurophysiological signatures, and dreaming had a physiological correlate that could be detected from outside the sleeper's skull. The paper is, considered as scientific writing, modest. It does not propose a model of dream formation. It does not speculate on function. It reports a phenomenon, gives a method, runs a control, and stops. What it leaves on the table is the implication: if dreaming is concentrated in a particular phase of sleep with a particular neural signature, then dreaming is now, in principle, a phenomenon accessible to laboratory study.
What followed within a decade

The shape of the field that grew up after 1953 is not in the paper itself but is implicit in its closing sentence about "this method furnishes the means of determining the incidence and duration of periods of dreaming." Within two years of the publication, William Dement — then a graduate student in the same Chicago laboratory — had used the method to establish (1955, 1957) that subjects woken during these eye-movement periods reliably recalled vivid dream content, while those woken at other points in the night did not. By the late 1960s, REM sleep had been worked out in considerable physiological detail: Michel Jouvet at Lyon had demonstrated the descending muscle atonia that paralyzes the body during REM, the cycling pattern through the night had been mapped, the role of the brainstem in initiating and terminating REM episodes had been outlined. By the late 1970s, when the first laboratory work on lucid dreaming was conducted, the framework was ready. There was a known phase of sleep in which dreaming was concentrated, accessible to electrophysiological recording. What was missing was a way to study the dreamer *as the dream was happening*. Subjective reports collected after waking were the only available source of information about what the dreamer had experienced. The reports were known to be unreliable in particular ways: forgetting, post-hoc reconstruction, the difficulty of putting dream-experience into the categories of waking language. There was no obvious way to address the problem. And then there was.
II. The signal-verified lucid dream (1978–1980)
The state of the question, late 1970s

The contemplative tradition's claim that dream-awareness is real and trainable had not been tested in a laboratory because it was not, in the 1970s, generally believed in the laboratory. LaBerge writes plainly about the climate in which he started his dissertation work at Stanford, and the passage is worth quoting because it captures the position in a single sentence:

"In the late 1970s, when I began my Ph.D. study on lucid dreams at Stanford, I found myself challenged by a seemingly even more hopeless task: proving that lucid dreaming is real. The experts at the time were convinced that dreaming with consciousness that you were dreaming was a contradiction in terms and therefore impossible. Such philosophical reasoning could not convince me, since I had experienced lucid dreams — impossible or not." (LaBerge and Rheingold, *Exploring the World of Lucid Dreaming*)

The objection LaBerge mentions — that consciousness within sleep is a contradiction in terms — was widely held in the dream-research community of the 1970s. It had its sources both in the dominant Hobson-McCarley activation-synthesis framework, which held that REM dreaming was driven by random pontine activation that the cortex synthesized into apparent narrative without higher-order monitoring, and in the older clinical tradition that treated lucidity reports as confabulation, brief micro-awakenings, or pathology. The objection was not stupid. It rested on a real difficulty: a person reporting after waking that they had been aware of dreaming might be reporting a dream-content (a dreamed awareness, not an actual one), or a memory-error (the awareness arising during the report rather than during the dream), or a brief awakening that had been forgotten. Without independent evidence of the awareness's location *inside* a confirmed dream, the report was, scientifically, not strong enough to settle the question.

What was needed was a way for the dreamer, while in the dream, to do something that could be detected on the polygraph trace and that no person not actually lucid in the dream could plausibly produce. The trick was that the body in REM is paralyzed by descending inhibition from the brainstem; the sleeper cannot speak, cannot move arms or legs, cannot act in any of the ways that ordinary outside-the-dream signals would use. Two muscle groups, however, are not paralyzed: the muscles of respiration (the sleeper continues to breathe) and the extraocular muscles (which is what gives REM its name in the first place). The eye movements that define REM sleep are, in principle, available for voluntary use by a dreamer who is aware enough to use them.
Hearne and Worsley at Hull

The first laboratory verification of lucid dreaming was conducted by Keith Hearne at the University of Hull in 1975, working with the experienced lucid dreamer Alan Worsley. The verification appeared in Hearne's 1978 PhD thesis ("Lucid Dreams: An Electro-Physiological and Psychological Study," University of Liverpool/Hull) and in subsequent journal publications. The Hearne thesis itself is not in the project source bundle; what we have is the discussion in LaBerge and Rheingold's book, which is generous and unambiguous. LaBerge credits Worsley as "the first person to signal from a lucid dream in pioneering experiments carried out in collaboration with Keith Hearne" (LaBerge and Rheingold, *Exploring the World of Lucid Dreaming*) and describes Worsley as "one of the world's most experienced lucid dreamers," who "has been conducting personal lucid dream experiments since the age of five." The Hearne-Worsley demonstration came in April 1975 in the Hull sleep laboratory: Worsley, while in confirmed REM sleep, executed a pre-arranged sequence of horizontal eye movements that had been agreed in waking, and the signal appeared on the polygraph trace.

The bundle does not contain Hearne's primary text, and so the present essay relies on LaBerge-Rheingold's discussion. What that discussion makes clear is that Hearne's experiment and LaBerge's later one are structurally the same experiment — different subjects, different laboratories, different sides of the Atlantic, but the same experimental insight and the same eye-signal protocol. Hearne's claim to scientific priority is firm. The fact that for several decades it was clouded by the earlier journal publication of LaBerge's parallel work is one of the small ironies of contemporary intellectual history; the present consensus is that Hearne and LaBerge did the same thing independently within a few years of each other, and both deserve the credit for the demonstration.
LaBerge at Stanford

LaBerge's own demonstration came in 1980 and is described in considerable detail in *Exploring the World of Lucid Dreaming.* The relevant passage opens with what LaBerge calls a poetic challenge — Coleridge's flower brought back from heaven — and then redescribes the challenge in operational terms. "I had no doubt that lucid dreaming was a reality, but how could I prove it to anyone else? To do so I needed to bring back evidence from the dream world as proof that I had really known I was dreaming during sleep. Simply reporting I had been lucid in a dream after awakening wouldn't prove that the lucidity had occurred while I was actually asleep. I needed some way to mark the time of the lucid dream on a record showing that I had been asleep" (LaBerge and Rheingold).

The insight that solved the problem was, like Hearne's, the recognition that REM eye movements were not paralyzed and that some of those eye movements during ordinary REM had previously been shown — in work by Dement and others — to correspond in direction with the gaze of the dreamer in the dream:

"I knew that earlier studies had demonstrated that the direction of dreamers' physical eye movements during REM was sometimes exactly the same as the direction that they reported looking in their dreams. In one remarkable example reported by pioneer sleep and dream researcher Dr. William Dement, a dreamer was awakened from REM sleep after making a series of about two dozen regular left-right-left-right eye movements. He reported that he was dreaming about a table tennis game; just before awakening he had been watching a long volley with his dream gaze. I also knew from my own experience that I could look in any direction I wished while in a lucid dream, so it occurred to me that I ought to be able to signal while I was having a lucid dream by moving my eyes in a pre-arranged, recognizable pattern." (LaBerge and Rheingold)

The Stanford experiment is then described almost summarily, because the substance of the experiment is the prior reasoning rather than the procedural execution. LaBerge spent the night at the Stanford Sleep Laboratory, wired with electrodes that "measured my brain waves, eye movements, and muscle tone, which my colleague Dr. Lynn Nagel monitored on a polygraph while I slept." During that night LaBerge had a lucid dream "in which I moved my eyes left-right-left-right." The next morning the polygraph record was found to contain the eye-movement signals "in the middle of a REM period." The result was published as LaBerge, Nagel, Dement, and Zarcone, "Lucid Dreaming Verified by Volitional Communication during REM Sleep," *Perceptual and Motor Skills* 52 (1981): 727–732 — the first peer-reviewed publication of an objectively verified lucid dream in a major journal. LaBerge's 1980 Stanford dissertation, *Lucid Dreaming: An Exploratory Study of Consciousness during Sleep,* is the foundational scientific document for the demonstration.
What was at stake in the demonstration

The demonstration's importance is more than the establishment of a single fact. Once the eye-signal paradigm was working, it became the methodological key to a research program. As LaBerge writes: "This method of communication from the dream world has proven to be of inestimable value in the continued study of lucid dreams and dream physiology. The fact that lucid dreamers could remember to perform previously agreed upon actions in their dreams and that they could signal to the waking world made an entirely new approach to dream research possible. By using trained lucid dreamers, we were able to develop the eye movement signaling technique into a powerful methodology" (LaBerge and Rheingold).

The book describes a range of follow-on experiments that the methodology made possible: time estimation in the dream (subjects were asked to signal, count to ten by a standard protocol, then signal again — and "in all cases, we found time estimates made in lucid dreams were within a few seconds of estimates made in the waking state"); the correspondence between dreamed gaze and physical eye movement; the physiology of dreamed action (when lucid dreamers held their breath in the dream "they really do hold their breaths," and when they breathed fast in the dream "they really do pant"); and a series of studies on the physiological correlates of dreamed sexual activity in lucid REM. Each of these required the eye-signal protocol as the marking-method. The signal told the experimenter where in the polygraph trace the dream-event of interest was. Without it, no such fine-grained correlation between dream content and physiology could have been established.

The wider implication was philosophical as much as methodological. The experts had said that consciousness within dreaming was a contradiction in terms. The signal said it was not. After 1980 this was no longer in scientific dispute. It is, in retrospect, hard to overstate the significance of the moment. For two thousand years, the contemplative traditions had insisted that lucidity was real, trainable, and central to a developed practice. For perhaps a hundred years, the Western lucid-dream literature (Saint-Denys, van Eeden, Ouspensky, Fox) had reported the phenomenon in detail. None of this had been received in the laboratory. Two pre-arranged eye movements changed that. As LaBerge puts it without flourish, "the fact that lucid dreamers could remember to perform previously agreed upon actions in their dreams and that they could signal to the waking world made an entirely new approach to dream research possible."
III. Techniques: MILD, WILD, WBTB

The eye-signal demonstration established that lucid dreaming was real. It did not, on its own, establish that lucid dreaming was reliably trainable. The early experimental subjects — LaBerge himself, Worsley, a small handful of other naturally adept lucid dreamers — were already capable of becoming lucid in the laboratory. The next question, and the one that would define LaBerge's career through the 1980s and 1990s, was whether the rest of the population could be taught to do the same thing. The development of induction techniques — methods by which an ordinary person, by deliberate practice, could increase the frequency of lucid dreaming from once a year or never to several times a week — is the part of the story that connects most directly to the curriculum. Three techniques came out of this period of work, and all three are described in *Exploring the World of Lucid Dreaming* in enough detail to be quoted directly.
MILD: Mnemonic Induction of Lucid Dreams

The first and most thoroughly developed of the techniques is LaBerge's own. The method emerged from his dissertation work; he had been using a vague autosuggestion method ("Tonight, I will have a lucid dream") with modest success — about five lucid dreams a month — but he could not reliably produce a lucid dream on a chosen night, and so he could not reliably book the laboratory for a confirming session. The MILD technique was developed by reflecting on what was actually happening when the autosuggestion worked:

"Nevertheless, I gradually observed a psychological factor that correlated with the occurrence of my lucid dreams: the presleep intention to remember to recognize I was dreaming. Once I knew how I was trying to induce lucid dreams, it became much easier to focus my efforts. This clarification of intention was followed by an immediate increase in the monthly frequency of my lucid dreams. Further practice and refinements led to a method whereby I could reliably induce lucid dreams. With this new method, I had as many as four lucid dreams in one night and as many as twenty-six in one month. I now could have a lucid dream on any night I chose and had accomplished my goal of showing that it is possible to bring access to the lucid dream state under volitional control." (LaBerge and Rheingold)

The conceptual reframing is the substance of the technique. Instead of trying to produce a lucid dream directly, the practitioner trains *prospective memory:* the capacity to remember, at a future time, to perform a specific action. In ordinary waking life, prospective memory is what allows a person to remember to buy tacks at the store, take a medication at noon, or call someone after a meeting. It runs on two factors: motivation (genuine intent fixes a target in memory) and association (a cue tied to the circumstances in which the action will need to be done). LaBerge's claim is that the dream-task — recognizing, while dreaming, that one is dreaming — has the same structure as any other prospective-memory task, and can be trained the same way. "Thinking of lucid dream induction as a problem of prospective memory, I developed a technique designed to increase my chances of remembering my intention to be lucid: the Mnemonic Induction of Lucid Dreams procedure, (MILD)" (LaBerge and Rheingold).
The technique itself, as given in the book, is a five-step procedure to be followed on awakening from a dream during the night:

"1. Set up dream recall. Before going to bed resolve to wake up and recall dreams during each dream period throughout the night. 2. Recall your dream. When you awaken from a dream period, no matter what time it is, try to recall as many details as possible from your dream. If you find yourself so drowsy that you are drifting back to sleep, do something to arouse yourself. 3. Focus your intent. While returning to sleep, concentrate single-mindedly on your intention to remember to recognize that you're dreaming. Tell yourself: 'Next time I'm dreaming, I want to remember I'm dreaming.' Really try to feel that you mean it. Narrow your thoughts to this idea alone. 4. See yourself becoming lucid. At the same time, imagine that you are back in the dream from which you have just awakened, but this time you recognize that it is a dream. Find a dreamsign in the experience; when you see it say to yourself: 'I'm dreaming!' and continue your fantasy. 5. Repeat. Repeat Steps 3 and 4 until your intention is set, then let yourself fall asleep." (LaBerge and Rheingold)

The 2018 PLOS ONE paper, working twenty-eight years later, restates the technique compactly enough to be useful as a second pass: "The purpose of MILD is to increase the frequency of lucid dreams using the intention to remember to do something in the future, a behavior now termed prospective memory. … One begins by remembering a dream, either from a previous night or, ideally, from which one has just awakened. One then identifies an anomaly within the dream that is characteristic of one's dreams (called a 'dreamsign'). Then, one visualizes returning to the dream, and rehearses recognizing that this anomaly only occurs in dreams, thereby becoming lucid. During this visualization, the practitioner mentally recites, 'The next time I'm dreaming I want to remember to recognize that I'm dreaming.' In addition, the technique involves visualizing carrying out a preselected action when one becomes lucid. A meditative focus is required to use MILD effectively" (LaBerge, LaMarca, and Baird 2018,).

The original 1980 dissertation established that MILD was, in LaBerge's own self-experimentation, "approximately 800% more effective than autosuggestion for inducing signal-verified lucid dreams in the sleep laboratory" (LaBerge 1980, summarized in the 2018 paper). LaBerge's account in *Exploring the World* is more concrete. With autosuggestion, he had had a lucid dream "on only one out of six nights in the lab"; with MILD, he had "one or more lucid dreams on twenty out of twenty-one nights spent in the sleep laboratory" (LaBerge and Rheingold). Subsequent studies, including the Aspy, Delfabbro, Proeve and Mohr 2017 trial cited in the 2018 paper, have confirmed that MILD increases lucid-dream frequency in motivated populations. The technique has its prerequisites — the practitioner must first develop reliable dream recall, and the book gives a one-week prospective-memory training exercise as a precondition for attempting MILD itself — but in motivated subjects it works, and it is, alongside WBTB, the most consistently effective technique in the experimental literature.
WILD: Wake-Initiated Lucid Dream

A second class of induction technique runs in the opposite direction. Where MILD aims to *carry an intention from the waking state into a dream that will arise spontaneously,* WILD aims to *carry awareness directly across the threshold of sleep* — the practitioner falls asleep without losing consciousness, and the awareness is therefore present in the resulting dream by continuity. LaBerge gives the operational distinction crisply:

"This chapter presents a completely different set of approaches to the world of lucid dreaming based on the idea of falling asleep consciously. This involves retaining consciousness while wakefulness is lost and allows direct entry into the lucid dream state without any loss of reflective consciousness. The basic idea has many variations. While falling asleep, you can focus on hypnagogic (sleep onset) imagery, deliberate visualizations, your breath or heartbeat, the sensations in your body, your sense of self, and so on. If you keep the mind sufficiently active while the tendency to enter REM sleep is strong, you feel your body fall asleep, but you, that is to say, your consciousness, remains awake. The next thing you know, you will find yourself in the dream world, fully lucid." (LaBerge and Rheingold)

The terminology of "WILD" versus "DILD" (dream-initiated lucid dream) is LaBerge's own; the underlying distinction had been observed in the contemplative literature for centuries. The Tibetan dream-yoga tradition has long emphasized the threshold of sleep as the operative training site (see Unit 2 on Namkhai Norbu's "practice of natural light" and on the throat-chakra visualizations, with which LaBerge's WILD techniques are explicitly continuous). LaBerge's three principal variants are: attention on hypnagogic imagery (the practitioner watches the half-formed images of sleep onset until they coalesce into a dream and then steps into it); attention on visualization, including the white-dot and black-dot techniques drawn directly from the Evans-Wentz translation of "The Doctrine of the Dream State"; and attention on the body, including the counting-to-sleep techniques and the various out-of-body-style methods grouped in chapter 4.

The empirical record on WILD is more equivocal than on MILD. WILDs are less common than DILDs in laboratory data — about 28 percent of laboratory lucid dreams in the studies LaBerge and Rheingold cite, against 72 percent DILDs — and considerably less common in home settings, where LaBerge's own home record had WILDs at only 5 percent. The technique requires more concentrated effort than MILD and is "much favored by the Tibetan tradition" precisely because it is the technique that depends on and develops a continuous quality of attention through the descent into sleep. Its rewards, when it works, are substantial: WILDs are typically more vivid and longer-lasting than DILDs, and the practitioner enters the dream already aware. As LaBerge writes, citing Tholey, "while techniques for direct entry to the dream state require considerable practice in the beginning, they offer correspondingly great rewards. When mastered, these techniques (like MILD) can confer the capacity to induce lucid dreams virtually at will" (LaBerge and Rheingold).
WBTB: Wake Back to Bed

The third technique is the most prosaic and, in motivated subjects, the most reliable single intervention in the literature. The reasoning behind WBTB is straightforward and is given in the book under the heading "Scheduling Time for Lucid Dreaming." Two facts about sleep architecture matter. First, the REM episodes get longer through the night: ninety minutes between REM periods at the start of the night, perhaps twenty or thirty minutes between them by the morning, and the late-night REM periods themselves are correspondingly longer. Second, lucidity is more likely in the long late-night REM periods than in the short early-night ones — partly because the brain in late-night REM is closer in activation profile to the waking state, and partly because the morning sleeper is closer to natural waking and so more easily holds attention through sleep onset. The WBTB protocol takes advantage of both facts. The practitioner sleeps for several hours, wakes, stays awake for thirty to ninety minutes (engaged in some quiet activity related to lucid dreaming — reading, journaling, MILD rehearsal), then returns to bed. The next REM episode is typically reached within a few minutes of falling asleep again, and that REM episode is unusually likely to contain a lucid dream.
LaBerge gives the technique as a five-step exercise:

"1. Set your alarm. Before going to bed, set your alarm to awaken you two to three hours earlier than usual, and go to sleep at your normal time. 2. Get out of bed promptly in the morning. When your alarm goes off, get out of bed immediately. You are going to stay awake for two or three hours. 3. Focus on your intentions for your lucid dreams. For the half hour before you return to sleep think about what you want to accomplish in your lucid dream. … 4. Return to bed and practice an induction technique. … 5. Give yourself at least two hours to sleep. … The morning hours are ideal for lucid dreaming for another reason. Although it takes us an hour to an hour and a half to get to REM sleep at the start of the night, after several hours of sleep we often can enter into REM only a few minutes after having been awake." (LaBerge and Rheingold)

Subsequent experimental work has confirmed the value of the protocol. The 2018 PLOS ONE paper notes that LaBerge, Phillips and Levitan's 1994 study (in *NightLight*, the Lucidity Institute's bulletin) had already established that "an hour of wakefulness before morning naps makes lucidity more likely" (LaBerge 2018, citing reference 18); subsequent work showed that "combining MILD with a longer period of wakefulness of either 30 to 60 minutes late in the sleep cycle was associated with even higher increases in lucid dream frequency in the ensuing sleep period" (LaBerge 2018). The Aspy, Delfabbro, Proeve and Mohr 2017 study (the National Australian Lucid Dream Induction Study), referenced by LaBerge 2018 as reference 19, showed that "participants who practiced a combination of MILD and sleep interruption nightly for two weeks demonstrated increased lucid dreaming frequency" (LaBerge 2018).

The current consensus, then, is that the three techniques are not alternatives but components of a single integrated protocol. WBTB sets up the sleep state in which lucidity is more likely; MILD provides the cognitive set during the wake period; and the dream that follows is then either a DILD (lucidity arises within the dream, triggered by a dreamsign) or, less commonly, a WILD (the practitioner returns to sleep without losing awareness). The Tholey reflection technique — the day-long practice of asking "Am I dreaming?" combined with a reality test, developed in Frankfurt by Paul Tholey beginning in the late 1950s — operates upstream of all three; it builds the meta-cognitive habit on which MILD's prospective-memory rehearsal and the dream-state recognition both depend.

A final note on Tholey, since the OUTLINE asks for him and the bundle's only access to him is through LaBerge-Rheingold's discussion. LaBerge introduces Tholey early — "the German psychologist Paul Tholey, who been studying lucid dreams for the past twenty years" — and treats him with consistent respect throughout the book. The reflection technique is described in detail (chapter 3, on critical state testing); Tholey's data on hostile dream figures is cited in the nightmare chapter; Tholey's induction techniques for direct dream entry are credited explicitly in the WILD chapter. The book is straightforward about its dependency on Tholey's prior work: "Paul Tholey has recently derived several techniques for inducing lucid dreams from over a decade of research involving more than two hundred subjects. Tholey claims that an effective method for achieving lucidity (especially for beginners) is to develop a 'critical-reflective attitude' toward your state of consciousness. This is done by asking yourself whether or not you are dreaming while you are awake. He stresses the importance of asking the 'critical question' ('Am I dreaming or not?') as frequently as possible, at least five to ten times a day, and in every situation that seems dreamlike" (LaBerge and Rheingold). The reflection technique as taught in contemporary courses is essentially Tholey's, with adjustments.
IV. Pharmacological induction (2018)
What the 2018 paper established

The pharmacological line of research has run alongside the cognitive-behavioral one since the late 1990s, with much of the early work conducted at the Lucidity Institute in Hawaii under LaBerge's direction.
